title: Sliding-Window Attention
description: An attention variant that restricts each query to a fixed local window of key positions instead of the full sequence.
openingSummary: Sliding-window attention is an attention variant that limits each token to a fixed local neighborhood, trading full-sequence reach for bounded compute and memory on long contexts.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Sliding-window attention is an attention variant that limits each query position to keys within a fixed neighborhood. Instead of a full n-by-n attention matrix, only entries inside the window receive non-zero weight.

whyItExists.title: Why It Exists
whyItExists.body: Sliding-window attention targets attention compute, memory bandwidth for score materialization, and the cost of serving long sequences when dense all-pairs attention would dominate runtime.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: Each query position attends only to keys within W tokens to its left (and optionally right, depending on the pattern). Keys outside the window are zeroed before softmax normalization, so the model spends compute on local context rather than the full token grid.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: With sequence length n and window size W, sliding-window attention applies a band mask before softmax. The formulas below contrast dense multi-head attention against window-limited attention that restricts which keys each query can reach.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with multi-head attention, sliding-window attention limits reach to a local neighborhood rather than scoring all positions. Multi-query and grouped-query attention remain dense over allowed positions but reduce key-value head count. Sliding-window attention instead changes how far each query can look.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Sliding-window attention appears in long-context decoder models that combine local windows with periodic global layers or other patterns to recover distant dependencies.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: A fixed window cannot directly connect distant tokens in a single layer. Models that rely only on local windows may need stacked layers or companion patterns to move information across the full sequence.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: As sequence lengths grow, quadratic attention cost motivates locality patterns that preserve useful nearby signal while avoiding a full n-by-n score matrix on every layer.

computeFlow.alt: Dense multi-head attention versus sliding-window local reach

window.attentionLocality: Fixed local window of W tokens per query
window.computeScaling: Linear O(n·W) per head when W is fixed
window.globalReach: Distant tokens require stacked layers or companion patterns

mha.attentionLocality: Full sequence; every query sees every key
mha.globalReach: Any position can attend to any other position

mqa.attentionLocality: Full sequence with shared key-value heads
mqa.computeScaling: Quadratic O(n²) with fewer key-value projections
mqa.globalReach: Any position can attend to any other position

gqa.attentionLocality: Full sequence with grouped key-value heads
gqa.computeScaling: Quadratic O(n²) with reduced key-value head count
gqa.globalReach: Any position can attend to any other position

g.definition: Window size: maximum token distance each query may attend to.

gi.definition: Band mask that zeroes key positions outside the sliding window.

mhaKvHeads.label: H key-value head pairs (1:1 with queries)

mhaOneToOne.label: Each query attends to every key position

windowLimitedReach.label: Attend only to keys inside the window

windowReachNote.label: Each query only reaches nearby keys inside the sliding window
